title.zh: 卷五十五 章帝八王傳
title.en: Volume 55: Biographies of Eight Princes of Emperor Zhang
title.raw: 卷五十五 章帝八王傳 Volume 55: Biographies of Eight Princes of Emperor Zhang

sentences.zh: 千乘貞王伉

translations.literal: Prince Zhen of Qiansheng, Kang.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Zhen of Qiansheng, Liu Kang.

sentences.zh: 千乘貞王伉，建初四年封。

translations.literal: Prince Zhen of Qiansheng, Kang, was enfeoffed in the fourth year of Jianchu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Kang, posthumously honored as Prince Zhen of Qiansheng, received his fief in 79 CE, the fourth year of the Jianchu era.

sentences.zh: 和帝即位，以伉長兄，甚見尊禮。

translations.literal: When Emperor He took the throne, because Kang was the eldest brother, he was greatly shown honor and courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor He came to the throne, he treated Kang, as his eldest brother, with exceptional respect.

sentences.zh: 立十五年薨。

translations.literal: Having been established fifteen years, he died.
translations.idiomatic: He held his title for fifteen years, then died.

sentences.zh: 子寵嗣，一名伏胡。

translations.literal: His son Chong succeeded; he had another name, Fuhu.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Chong inherited the title; he was also known as Fuhu.

sentences.zh: 永元七年，改國名樂安。

translations.literal: In the seventh year of Yongyuan, the name of the state was changed to Le'an.
translations.idiomatic: In 95 CE, the seventh year of Yongyuan, his domain was renamed the Principality of Le'an.

sentences.zh: 立二十八年薨，是為夷王。

translations.literal: Having been established twenty-eight years, he died; this one is Prince Yi.
translations.idiomatic: After twenty-eight years as prince he died and is remembered posthumously as Prince Yi.

sentences.zh: 父子薨於京師，皆葬洛陽。

translations.literal: Father and son died in the capital; both were buried at Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Both father and son died at the capital and were interred at Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 子鴻嗣。

translations.literal: His son Hong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Hong succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 安帝崩，始就國。

translations.literal: When Emperor An died, he only then went to his state.
translations.idiomatic: It was only after Emperor An's death that he took up residence in his fief.

sentences.zh: 鴻生質帝，質帝立，梁太后下詔，以樂安國土卑濕，租委鮮薄，改[封]鴻*

translations.literal: Hong begat Emperor Zhi. When Zhi was enthroned, Empress Dowager Liang issued an edict on the grounds that the Territory of Le'an was low-lying and damp and its rents and conveyed grain were scant, to change the enfeoffment of Hong
translations.idiomatic: Liu Hong was the father of Emperor Zhi. After Zhi mounted the throne, Empress Dowager Liang promulgated an edict citing Le'an's soggy lowlands and meager tax yield, and ordered Hong's title transferred

translations.literal: (enfeoffment)
translations.idiomatic: (to a new enfeoffment)

sentences.zh: *勃海王。

translations.literal: to Prince of Bohai.
translations.idiomatic: to that of Prince of Bohai.

sentences.zh: [一]立二十六年薨，是為孝王。

translations.literal: [One] Having been established twenty-six years, he died; this one is Prince Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: He reigned twenty-six years and died, receiving the posthumous title Prince Xiao (the Filial).

sentences.zh: 注[一]委謂委輸也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Wei means conveyed transport.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Here wei denotes grain and goods shipped in as tribute or tax.

sentences.zh: 無子，太后立桓帝弟蠡吾侯悝為勃海王，奉鴻*

translations.literal: Having no son, the empress dowager established Emperor Huan's younger brother, Marquis of Liyi, Kui, as Prince of Bohai, to continue Hong's
translations.idiomatic: Hong left no son, so the empress dowager named Emperor Huan's younger brother, the Marquis of Liyi Liu Kui, Prince of Bohai to carry on Hong's

translations.literal: (succession)
translations.idiomatic: (line of succession)

translations.literal: **[sacrifices]*.
translations.idiomatic: ancestral cult.

sentences.zh: [一]延熹八年，悝謀為不道，有司請廢之。

translations.literal: [One] In the eighth year of Yanxi, Kui plotted conduct contrary to the Way; the responsible officials requested that he be deposed.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: In 165 CE, the eighth year of Yanxi, Liu Kui was accused of treasonous plotting, and the authorities petitioned to strip him of his rank.

sentences.zh: 帝不忍，乃貶為廮陶王，食一縣。

translations.literal: The emperor could not bear it; thereupon he was demoted to Prince of Yingtao, eating one county.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor relented and merely demoted him to Prince of Yingtao with an income drawn from a single county.

sentences.zh: 注[一]悝，蠡吾侯翼子，河閒王開孫也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Kui was a son of Marquis of Liyi Yi and a grandson of Prince of Hejian Kai.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Liu Kui was the son of Liu Yi, Marquis of Liyi, and a grandson of Liu Kai, Prince of Hejian.

sentences.zh: 悝後因中常侍王甫求復國，許謝錢五千萬。

translations.literal: Kui afterward, by means of Palace Attendant Wang Fu, sought restoration of his state and promised thanks-money of fifty million cash.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Kui later bribed the eunuch Wang Fu to lobby for the restoration of his principality, pledging fifty million cash in gratitude.

sentences.zh: 帝臨崩，遺詔復為勃海王。

translations.literal: When the emperor was on the point of death, he left a testamentary edict restoring him as Prince of Bohai.
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed the emperor left orders restoring Kui as Prince of Bohai.

sentences.zh: 悝知非甫功，不肯還謝錢。

translations.literal: Kui knew it was not Fu's achievement and was unwilling to return the thanks-money.
translations.idiomatic: Kui knew Wang Fu had not truly earned the favor and refused to pay the promised sum.

sentences.zh: 甫怒，陰求其過。

translations.literal: Fu was angry and secretly sought his faults.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Fu, furious, began quietly digging for dirt on him.

sentences.zh: 初，迎立靈帝，道路流言悝恨不得立，欲鈔征書。

translations.literal: At first, when welcoming the establishment of Emperor Ling, on the roads were spreading words that Kui resented not having been established and wished to intercept the summons.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, when Emperor Ling was chosen for the throne, rumor had it that Kui resented being passed over and meant to seize the imperial summons on the road.

sentences.zh: 而中常侍鄭颯、[一]中黃門董騰並任俠通剽輕，數與悝交通。

translations.literal: Moreover Palace Attendant Zheng Sa [one] and Yellow Gate Dong Teng together acted as knights-errant and associated with the swift and plundering; they repeatedly communicated with Kui.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile the eunuchs Zheng Sa and Dong Teng moved in swaggering, violent circles and were in frequent contact with Kui.

sentences.zh: [二] 王甫司察，以為有奸，密告司隸校尉段熲。

translations.literal: [Two] Wang Fu supervised investigation, considered there to be treachery, and secretly informed Colonel Director of Retainers Duan Jiong.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Wang Fu, overseeing the inquiry, decided there was a conspiracy and secretly denounced the matter to Duan Jiong, colonel director of retainers.

sentences.zh: 熹平元年，遂收颯送北寺獄。

translations.literal: In the first year of Xiping, thereupon Sa was seized and sent to Northern Office prison.
translations.idiomatic: In 172 CE, the first year of Xiping, Zheng Sa was arrested and thrown into the Northern Office jail.

sentences.zh: [三] 使尚書令廉忠誣奏颯等謀迎立悝，大逆不道。

translations.literal: [Three] He caused Minister Secretary Lian Zhong falsely to memorialize that Sa and others plotted to welcome and establish Kui, great rebellion contrary to the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: He had Minister Secretary Lian Zhong frame Zheng Sa and his associates for plotting to put Kui on the throne—a capital crime.

sentences.zh: 遂詔冀州刺史收悝考實，又遣大鴻臚持節與宗正、廷尉之勃海，迫責悝。

translations.literal: Thereupon he edicted the governor of Ji Province to seize Kui and examine the facts, and also sent the grand herald bearing credentials together with the director of the imperial clan and the commandant of justice to Bohai to press and blame Kui.
translations.idiomatic: An edict ordered the governor of Ji to take Kui into custody for questioning, while the grand herald, bearing the imperial staff, went to Bohai with the director of the imperial clan and the commandant of justice to interrogate him.

sentences.zh: 悝自殺。

translations.literal: Kui killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Kui took his own life.

sentences.zh: 妃妾十一人，子女七十人，伎女二十四人，皆死獄中。

translations.literal: Eleven consorts and concubines, seventy sons and daughters, twenty-four female entertainers—all died in prison.
translations.idiomatic: Eleven of his wives and concubines, seventy of his children, and twenty-four female performers all perished in custody.

sentences.zh: 傅、相以下，以輔導王不忠，悉伏誅。

translations.literal: From tutors and chancellors downward, because they had not been loyal in assisting and guiding the prince, all were executed.
translations.idiomatic: His tutors, chancellor, and subordinate officials were put to death for failing in their duty to counsel the prince faithfully.

sentences.zh: 悝立二十五年國除。

translations.literal: Kui having been established twenty-five years, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: After twenty-five years under Kui, the principality was extinguished.

sentences.zh: 觿庶莫不憐之。

translations.literal: There was none among the girdle-pendants and commoners who did not pity him.
translations.idiomatic: From gentlemen to common folk, everyone pitied him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]音立。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The sound is li.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The graph is read li (as in 立).

sentences.zh: 注[二]剽，疾也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Piao means swift.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Piao here means 'swift' or 'recklessly bold.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]北寺，獄名，屬黃門署。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Northern Office is a prison name; it belongs to the Yellow Gates office.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: The Northern Office was a prison under the eunuch directorate of the Yellow Gates.

sentences.zh: 前書音義曰即若盧獄也。

translations.literal: The Former Book's Sounds and Meanings says it is the same as the Ruolu prison.
translations.idiomatic: The Han shu commentary identifies it with the Ruolu prison of Western Han.

sentences.zh: 平春悼王全

translations.literal: Prince Dao of Pingchun, Quan.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Dao of Pingchun, Liu Quan.

sentences.zh: 平春悼王全，[一]以建初四年封。

translations.literal: Prince Dao of Pingchun, Quan, [one] in the fourth year of Jianchu was enfeoffed.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Quan, posthumously Prince Dao of Pingchun, was enfeoffed in 79 CE, the fourth year of Jianchu.

sentences.zh: 其年薨，葬於京師。

translations.literal: That year he died and was buried in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: He died the same year and was buried in the capital.

sentences.zh: 無子，國除。

translations.literal: Having no son, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: He had no heir, so the fief was struck off.

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢志平春，縣，屬江夏郡也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Continued Han Treatises state Pingchun is a county belonging to Jiangxia commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: According to the Xu Han zhi, Pingchun was a county in Jiangxia commandery.

sentences.zh: 清河孝王慶

translations.literal: Prince Xiao of Qinghe, Qing.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Xiao of Qinghe, Liu Qing.

sentences.zh: 注[一]昌，文帝時為中尉，以代邸功封壯武侯。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Chang, in Emperor Wen's time was commandant of the capital guards and, on account of merit at the Dai residence, was enfeoffed as marquis of Zhuangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Liu Chang served as metropolitan commandant under Emperor Wen and was made Marquis of Zhuangwu for his service when Wen was heir at Dai.

sentences.zh: 注[二]比陽主，東海王強女。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Princess of Biyang was a daughter of Prince of the East Sea Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The Princess of Biyang was a daughter of Liu Qiang, Prince of the East Sea.

sentences.zh: 注[三]偵，候也，音丑政反。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Zhen means to spy; the sound is chou zheng fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Zhen means 'to watch' or 'to reconnoiter,' read chou-zheng (去聲).

sentences.zh: 廣雅曰：「偵，問也。」

translations.literal: The Guang ya states: 'Zhen means to inquire.'
translations.idiomatic: The Guang ya glosses zhen as 'to inquire' or 'to investigate.'

sentences.zh: 慶出居承祿觀，數月，竇後諷掖庭令誣奏前事，請加驗實。

translations.literal: Qing moved out to dwell at Chenglu Lodge; after several months, Empress Dou indirectly instructed the director of the Rear Palace falsely to memorialize the former affair and request adding verification of the facts.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Qing was moved to the Chenglu Lodge. Within months Empress Dou had the director of the imperial harem trump up the old charges and demand a formal inquiry.

sentences.zh: 七年，帝遂廢太子慶而立皇太子□。

translations.literal: In the seventh year, the emperor thereupon deposed Heir Apparent Qing and established Imperial Heir Apparent [lacuna in text].
translations.idiomatic: In the seventh year of his reign the emperor deposed Crown Prince Liu Qing and enthroned Liu Zhao as crown prince in his stead.

sentences.zh: □，梁貴人子也。

translations.literal: [Lacuna in text] was a son of the Honored Lady Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Zhao was the son of the Honored Lady Liang.

sentences.zh: 乃下詔曰：「皇太子有失惑無常之性，爰自孩乳，至今益章，恐襲其母凶惡之風，不可以奉宗廟，為天下主。

translations.literal: Thereupon he issued an edict saying: 'The imperial heir apparent has a nature of loss and confusion without constancy; from infant suckling to the present it has become increasingly manifest; I fear he will inherit his mother's fierce and evil wind; he cannot serve the ancestral temple or be lord of All under Heaven.'
translations.idiomatic: An edict followed: 'The crown prince is unsteady and erratic of temper; the trait has shown ever more clearly since his infancy. I fear he will take after his mother's vicious character. He is unfit to tend the imperial shrines or rule the realm.'

sentences.zh: 大義滅親，況降退乎！

translations.literal: Great righteousness destroys kin—how much more demotion and dismissal!
translations.idiomatic: When the greater good demands it, even kin may be set aside—how much more a mere demotion!'

sentences.zh: 注[一]左傳，□石碏殺其子厚，君子曰：「石碏純臣也，惡州吁而厚預焉。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Zuo Zhuan, [lacuna] Shi Que killed his son Hou; the noble man said: 'Shi Que was a pure minister; he hated Zhou Xu yet Hou participated therein.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The Zuo zhuan (Wei) records that Shi Que executed his son Hou; the gentleman remarks, 'Shi Que was a loyal minister: he abhorred Zhou Xu, yet Hou abetted him.'

sentences.zh: 大義滅親，其是之謂乎！」

translations.literal: Great righteousness destroys kin—is it not meant in this sense!'
translations.idiomatic: When duty to the state outweighs family ties—surely this is what was meant!'

sentences.zh: 注[二]儀禮喪服曰：「慈母如母。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Yili, Mourning Dress, states: 'A foster mother is like a mother.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The Yili, 'Mourning Garments,' says of a foster mother, 'Treat her as you would a mother.'

sentences.zh: 」謂妾子之無母，父命妾養之。

translations.literal: 'It means a concubine's son who has no mother; the father orders a concubine to rear him.'
translations.idiomatic: That is, when a concubine's son loses his birth mother, the father designates another concubine to raise him.

sentences.zh: 故曰慈母，如母者，貴父之命也。

translations.literal: Therefore it is called foster mother; 'like a mother' values the father's command.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the title 'foster mother': she is honored as a mother because the father's command invests her with that role.

sentences.zh: 注[三]傅讀曰附。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Fu is read as fu (attach).
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: The graph fu (tutor) is read here with the same sound as fu meaning 'to attach' (an attached tutor).

sentences.zh: 注[四]續漢志曰「暴室，署名，主中婦人疾病」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: The Continued Han Treatises state: 'Baoshi is an office name; it supervises diseases of palace women.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: The Xu Han zhi describes the Baoshi as an agency that tended ill women of the inner palace.

sentences.zh: 注[五]在洛陽城北也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: It is north of the wall of Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: It lay north of the Luoyang city wall.

sentences.zh: 後慶以長，別居丙捨。

translations.literal: Afterward Qing, because of seniority, separately dwelt in the Bing quarters.
translations.idiomatic: Later, as the eldest imperial son, Qing was lodged apart in the Bing apartments.

sentences.zh: 永元四年，帝移幸北宮章德殿，講於白虎觀，慶得入省宿止。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Yongyuan, the emperor shifted his favor to Zhangde Hall in the Northern Palace and lectured at White Tiger Lodge; Qing was able to enter and lodge on duty.
translations.idiomatic: In 92 CE, the fourth year of Yongyuan, the emperor took up residence in Zhangde Hall in the Northern Palace and held seminars at the White Tiger Lodge, which allowed Liu Qing to visit and stay overnight on duty.

sentences.zh: 帝將誅竇氏，欲得外戚傳，[一]懼左右不敢使，乃令慶私從千乘王求，夜獨內之；

translations.literal: The emperor was about to execute the Dou clan and wished to obtain the Treatise on the Outer Kin [one]; fearing those at his side, he did not dare dispatch them; thereupon he ordered Qing privately from the Prince of Qiansheng to seek it and alone at night brought it in;
translations.idiomatic: Planning to destroy the Dou faction, the emperor needed the Han shu's 'Treatise on the Outer Kin' but dared not send anyone near him for it, so he had Liu Qing obtain it secretly from the Prince of Qiansheng and slip it in alone after dark.

sentences.zh: 又令慶傳語中常侍鄭觿求索故事。

translations.literal: He also ordered Qing to transmit words to Palace Attendant Zheng Yi to seek precedents of old.
translations.idiomatic: He also told Liu Qing to ask the eunuch Zheng Yi to dig up relevant precedents.

sentences.zh: [二]及大將軍竇憲誅，慶出居邸，賜奴婢三百人，輿馬、錢帛、帷帳、珍寶、玩好充仞其第，又賜中傅以下至左右錢帛各有差。

translations.literal: [Two] When Grand General Dou Xian was executed, Qing moved out to dwell in a mansion; there were bestowed three hundred male and female slaves, carriages and horses, cash and silks, curtains and canopies, precious objects and playthings filling his residence; moreover from the palace tutor downward to those at left and right, cash and silks were bestowed each according to rank.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: After Grand General Dou Xian was put to death, Liu Qing moved to an outer residence. The throne showered him with three hundred slaves, carriages, horses, cash, silk, hangings, treasures, and curios until his house overflowed, and ranked gifts went to his tutors and household staff.

translations.literal: [Three]
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: (no text)

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書外戚傳也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: It is the Former Book's Treatise on the Outer Kin.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: That is, the 'Treatise on the Outer Kin' in the Han shu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謂文帝誅薄昭，武帝誅竇嬰故事。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: It means the precedents of Emperor Wen executing Bo Zhao and Emperor Wu executing Dou Ying.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The precedents of Emperor Wen's execution of his uncle Bo Zhao and Emperor Wu's execution of Dou Ying.

sentences.zh: 注[三]前書音義曰：「中傅，宦者也。」

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The Former Book's Sounds and Meanings state: 'The palace tutor is a eunuch.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: The Han shu commentary defines the palace tutor (zhong fu) as a eunuch official.

sentences.zh: 慶多被病，或時不安，帝朝夕問訊，進膳藥，所以垂意甚備。

translations.literal: Qing often suffered illness; at times he was unwell; the emperor morning and evening inquired after him, advanced food and medicines; the manner in which he let fall his mind was very complete.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Qing was often ill. The emperor visited him morning and evening, sent food and medicine, and showed every solicitude.

sentences.zh: 慶小心恭孝，自以廢黜，尤畏事慎法。

translations.literal: Qing was careful in mind, respectful and filial; because he himself had been deposed and dismissed, especially he feared affairs and was cautious of the law.
translations.idiomatic: Mindful of his deposition, Liu Qing lived with scrupulous deference and filial piety, ever fearful of giving offense and careful to stay within the law.

sentences.zh: 每朝謁陵廟，常夜分嚴裝，衣冠待明；

translations.literal: Whenever attending court or visiting tombs and temples, he constantly at midnight's division rigorously dressed, cap and robes waiting for dawn;
translations.idiomatic: Whenever he was to attend court or worship at the imperial tombs, he would rise in the dead of night, dress with care, and wait fully robed for dawn.

sentences.zh: [一]約□官屬，不得與諸王車騎競驅。

translations.literal: [One] He restrained [lacuna] officials and followers; they could not with the various princes' chariots and riders compete in galloping.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: He kept his entourage under strict orders not to race their carriages against those of other princes.

sentences.zh: 常以貴人葬禮有闕，每竊感恨，至四節伏臘，輒祭於私室。

translations.literal: He constantly because the Honored Lady's funeral rites had deficiencies, every time secretly felt grief and resentment; reaching the four seasons' Fu and La, then he sacrificed in his private chamber.
translations.idiomatic: He never ceased to grieve that his mother's obsequies had been slighted, and at each seasonal offering—summer and winter—he would worship her privately in his rooms.

sentences.zh: 竇氏誅後，始使乳母於城北遙祠。

translations.literal: After the Dou clan was executed, he only then caused his wet nurse at the north of the city distantly to sacrifice.
translations.idiomatic: Only after the Dou clan fell could he send his nurse to offer distant sacrifice north of the city.

sentences.zh: 及竇太后崩，慶求上頤致哀，帝許之，詔太官四時給祭具。

translations.literal: When Empress Dowager Dou died, Qing requested to go up to the mausoleum to express grief; the emperor permitted it and edicted the grand provisioner at the four seasons to supply sacrificial implements.
translations.idiomatic: When Empress Dowager Dou died, Liu Qing asked leave to mourn at her tomb; the emperor agreed and ordered the imperial kitchen to furnish seasonal offerings.

sentences.zh: 慶垂涕曰：「生雖不獲供養，終得奉祭祀，私願足矣。

translations.literal: Qing, tears falling, said: 'In life although I did not obtain to support and nurture, in the end I obtain to perform the sacrifices; my private wish is sufficient.'
translations.idiomatic: With tears Liu Qing said, 'I could not care for her in life, but at least I may honor her in death. That is all I wished for.'

sentences.zh: 」欲求作祠堂，恐有自同恭懷梁後之嫌，遂不敢言。

translations.literal: 'He wished to seek to make an ancestral hall but feared there would be the suspicion of self-identifying with the Gonghuai Empress Liang; thereupon he did not dare speak.'
translations.idiomatic: He longed to build a shrine but feared being thought to rank himself with Empress Gonghuai of Liang, so he held his tongue.

sentences.zh: [二]常泣向左右，以為沒齒之恨。

translations.literal: [Two] He constantly wept toward left and right, considering it a regret to the end of his teeth.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: He wept often before his attendants, calling it a sorrow he would carry to the grave.

sentences.zh: [三]後上言外祖母王年老，遭憂病，下土無醫藥，願乞詣洛陽療疾。

translations.literal: [Three] Later he submitted words that his maternal grandmother Wang was old, had encountered grief illness, and in the lower land there were no physicians or medicines; he wished to beg to go to Luoyang to treat illness.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: He later memorialized that his maternal grandmother, the Lady Wang, was old and ill with no decent care in her home commandery, and asked permission to bring her to Luoyang for treatment.

sentences.zh: 於是詔宋氏悉歸京師，除慶舅衍、俊、蓋、暹等皆為郎。

translations.literal: Thereupon he edicted the Song clan entirely to return to the capital; he appointed Qing's maternal uncles Yan, Jun, Gai, Xian, and others all as gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: An edict then summoned the whole Song clan to the capital and named Liu Qing's uncles Yan, Jun, Gai, Xian, and the rest gentlemen of the palace.

sentences.zh: 注[一]分，半也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Fen means half.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Fen here means 'the middle of the night' (half the night).

sentences.zh: 注[二]恭懷梁後，和帝母梁貴人。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Gonghuai Empress Liang was Emperor He's mother, the Honored Lady Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Empress Gonghuai of Liang was Emperor He's birth mother, the Honored Lady Liang.

sentences.zh: 注[三]沒，終；

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Mo means end;
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Mo means 'until the end';

sentences.zh: 齒，年也。

translations.literal: chi means year.
translations.idiomatic: chi means 'years' (of one's life).

sentences.zh: 十五年，有司以日食陰盛，奏遣諸王侯就國。

translations.literal: In the fifteenth year, the responsible officials, because of solar eclipse yin excess, memorialized dispatching the various princes and marquises to go to their states.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifteenth year of Yongyuan (103 CE), citing a solar eclipse as a sign of excessive yin influence, officials urged that the imperial princes be sent to their fiefs.

sentences.zh: 詔曰：「甲子之異，責由一人。

translations.literal: An edict stated: 'The anomaly of the jiazi day, the blame lies in one man.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor replied, 'Heaven's warning on the jiazi day is my fault alone.'

sentences.zh: 諸王幼稚，早離顧復，弱冠相育，[一]常有蓼莪、凱風之哀。

translations.literal: The various princes are young and tender; early they departed care and return; at the capping of twenty they mutually rear; [one] they constantly have the grief of 'Luxuriant Motherwort' and 'The Breeze from the South.'
translations.idiomatic: The princes are still boys, torn young from their parents' care and raised apart until their capping; they are entitled to the sorrow voiced in the 'Motherwort' and 'Southern Breeze' odes.

sentences.zh: [二]選懦之恩，知非國典，且復須留。

translations.literal: [Two] The grace of weak timidity, I know is not the state's norm; for the time being again must retain.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: To indulge my own soft heart would break standing law; for now they may stay.

sentences.zh: 」[三]至冬，從祠章陵，詔假諸王羽林騎各四十人。

translations.literal: '[Three] Reaching winter, following sacrifice at Zhangling, he edicted temporarily lending the various princes forty Feather Forest riders each.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: That winter, when the court went to sacrifice at Zhangling, he lent each prince forty guards from the Feather Forest corps for the journey.

sentences.zh: 後中傅□欣私為臧盜千餘萬，詔使案理之，並責慶不舉之狀，慶曰：「欣以師傅之尊，選自聖朝，臣愚唯知言從事聽，不甚有所愨察。

translations.literal: Later the palace tutor [lacuna] Xin privately committed embezzlement and theft of over ten million; an edict ordered investigation and judgment, and together blamed Qing for the circumstance of not having reported; Qing said: 'Xin, by the honor of tutor-master, was selected from the sage court; I, the minister, foolishly only knew to speak following affairs and listen; I did not greatly have what to examine with caution.'
translations.idiomatic: Later his palace tutor Xin embezzled more than ten million cash. The throne ordered an inquiry and reproached Liu Qing for not having denounced him. Liu Qing answered, 'Xin was my tutor, appointed by Your Majesty's grace. I am a dull man who merely obeyed his instructions and scarcely thought to spy on him.'

sentences.zh: 」帝嘉其對，悉以欣臧財賜慶。

translations.literal: The emperor praised his reply and bestowed on Qing the entirety of Xin's ill-gotten wealth.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved his answer and turned the whole of Xin's forfeited fortune over to Liu Qing.

sentences.zh: 及帝崩，慶號泣前殿，嘔血數升，因以發病。

translations.literal: When the emperor died, Qing wailed and wept in the front hall, vomited blood several sheng, and by this fell ill.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor died, Liu Qing sobbed in the front hall until he brought up several pints of blood and fell seriously ill.

sentences.zh: 注[一]詩小雅曰：「父兮生我，母兮鞠我，顧我復我，出入腹我。」

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Classic of Poetry, Minor Odes, states: 'O father, you begat me; O mother, you nourished me; you looked after me, you brought me back; going out and coming in, you embraced me.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: From the 'Minor Odes': 'Father, you gave me life; mother, you nursed me; you watched over me, you brought me home; you sheltered me wherever I went.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]詩小雅曰：「蓼蓼者莪，匪莪伊蒿。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Classic of Poetry, Minor Odes, states: 'Luxuriant is the motherwort, yet it is not motherwort but wormwood.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The 'Motherwort' ode runs, 'So tall the motherwort—yet no motherwort, only wormwood.'

sentences.zh: 哀哀父母，生我劬勞。

translations.literal: Alas alas father and mother, you bore me with toil and labor.'
translations.idiomatic: Alas for my father and mother, who gave me life in bitter toil.

sentences.zh: 」詩國風曰：「凱風自南，吹彼棘心。

translations.literal: 'The Classic of Poetry, Airs of the States, states: 'The gentle breeze from the south blows that jujube heart.'
translations.idiomatic: The 'Southern Breeze' ode says, 'The warm wind from the south bends the tender jujube heart.'

sentences.zh: 棘心夭夭，母氏劬勞。」

translations.literal: The jujube heart is tender and bright; the mother labors in bitter toil.'
translations.idiomatic: That tender jujube heart—what a mother endures for her sake.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]選懦，仁弱慈戀不決之意也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Xuanruo means the sense of benevolent weakness, fond attachment, and inability to decide.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Xuanruo describes excessive tenderness—reluctance to send the princes away out of misplaced pity.

sentences.zh: 懦音仁兗反。

translations.literal: Ruo has the reading rin yuan fan.
translations.idiomatic: The character ruo is read rin-yuan (上聲).

sentences.zh: 東觀記「須留」作「宿留」。

translations.literal: The Eastern Lodge Record writes xu liu as su liu.
translations.idiomatic: The Dongguan ji glosses the phrase as su liu ('to linger') instead of xu liu.

sentences.zh: 明年，諸王就國，鄧太后特聽清河王置中尉、內史，賜什物皆取乘輿上御，以宋衍等並為清河中大夫。

translations.literal: The next year the various princes went to their states; Empress Dowager Deng specially permitted the Prince of Qinghe to establish a commandant of attendants and an interior secretary; bestowed utensils were all taken from what rode above on the imperial equipage; Song Yan and others together were made grandees in the palace of Qinghe.
translations.idiomatic: The next year the princes left for their fiefs, but Empress Dowager Deng allowed the Prince of Qinghe alone to appoint a commandant of attendants and an interior secretary; his gifts were taken from the emperor's own carriage furnishings, and Song Yan and his fellows were all named grandees of the Qinghe household.

sentences.zh: [一]慶到國，下令：「寡人生於深宮，長於朝廷，[二] 仰恃明主，垂拱受成。

translations.literal: [One] When Qing arrived in the state he issued an order: 'The solitary one was born in the deep palace, grew up at court, [two] looked up and relied on the enlightened lord, hung his sash in ease, and received what was already accomplished.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: When Liu Qing reached his domain he issued a proclamation: 'I was born within the palace and raised at court, Note [2]: ever leaning on a perspicacious sovereign who ruled with folded hands while I simply accepted the ready-made order of things.'

sentences.zh: [三]既以薄佑，早離顧復，屬遭大憂，[四]悲懷感傷。

translations.literal: [Three] Already because of slight blessing, early departed care and return; having encountered great grief, [four] grief and sorrow wound the heart.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Heaven has granted me little favor—I was torn early from a parent's care—and then came crushing bereavement, Note [4]: so grief and longing have never left me.'

sentences.zh: 蒙恩大國，職惟藩輔，新去京師，憂心煢煢，夙夜屏營，未知所立。

translations.literal: Having received grace in a great state, my office is solely that of a frontier bulwark; newly departed from the capital, my anxious heart stands alone; night and day I pace in trepidation, not knowing what to uphold.
translations.idiomatic: I now hold a great fief as a bulwark of the throne, yet I have just left the capital; my heart is utterly alone, and night and day I tremble, unsure how to comport myself.'

sentences.zh: [五]蓋聞智不獨理，必須明賢。

translations.literal: [Five] I have heard that wisdom does not govern alone; it must have clarity and worth.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: I have heard that no wise man governs by himself—he needs worthy and discerning ministers.'

sentences.zh: 今官屬並居爵任，失得是均，庶望上遵策戒，下免悔咎。

translations.literal: Now officials and followers together dwell in rank and duty; gain and loss are alike shared; I hope above to follow the admonitions of the edicts, below to avoid the fault of regret.
translations.idiomatic: You who share my rank and duty—we rise or fall together. I look to you to heed the court's injunctions so that none of us need fear remorse.'

sentences.zh: 其愨督非枉，明察典禁，無令孤獲怠慢之罪焉。」

translations.literal: Earnestly supervise what is not crooked, clearly inspect the statutes and prohibitions; do not let the solitary one incur the crime of negligence.'
translations.idiomatic: Watch diligently for wrongdoing, keep a clear eye on the laws, and do not let me fall into the charge of slack rule.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢*

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Continued Han—
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The gloss cites the Xu Han shu (Continued Book of Han)—

translations.literal: —shu (book).
translations.idiomatic: —the graph shu (completing the title Xu Han shu).

sentences.zh: **[志]*曰：「中大夫，秩六百石，無員，掌奉王使至京師。」

translations.literal: **[treatise] states: "The grandee in the palace, rank six hundred bushels, without fixed quota, manages the king's missions to the capital."
translations.idiomatic: The [treatise] reads: "Palace grandees at six hundred bushels, without fixed numbers, conduct the prince's business at the capital."

sentences.zh: 注[二]魯哀公與孔子言曰：「寡人生於深宮之中，長於婦人之手。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Duke Ai of Lu spoke with Confucius saying: 'The solitary one was born in the deep palace, grew up in women's hands.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Duke Ai of Lu said to Confucius, 'I was born deep in the palace and raised in women's quarters.'

sentences.zh: 」事見孫卿子也。

translations.literal: 'The affair is seen in Master Xun.
translations.idiomatic: The story appears in the Xunzi.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]垂拱言無為也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Chui gong speaks of non-action.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: 'Hanging the sash' (chui gong) implies ruling without meddling.

sentences.zh: 尚書曰：「垂拱仰成。」

translations.literal: The Documents state: 'Hang the sash and look up to what is accomplished.'
translations.idiomatic: The Shang shu says, 'The ruler folds his hands while others bring the work to completion.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]屬，近。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Shu means near.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: Shu here means 'recently' or 'close at hand.'

sentences.zh: 注[五]煢煢，孤特也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Qiong qiong means solitary and alone.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: Qiong qiong means 'utterly alone.'

sentences.zh: 屏營，仿偟也。

translations.literal: Ping ying means pacing to and fro.
translations.idiomatic: Ping ying means 'pacing anxiously back and forth.'

sentences.zh: 鄧太后以殤帝襁抱，遠慮不虞，[一]留慶長子佑與嫡母耿姬居清河邸。

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Deng, because Emperor Shang was in swaddling clothes, far-considered unanticipated events, [one] retained Qing's eldest son You and his principal mother Lady Geng Ji to dwell in the Qinghe mansion in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Because the infant Emperor Shang needed protection, Empress Dowager Deng kept Liu Qing's eldest son You and his lawful mother Lady Geng at the Qinghe mansion in Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 至秋，帝崩，立佑為嗣，是為安帝。

translations.literal: When autumn came the emperor died; You was established as successor; this is Emperor An.
translations.idiomatic: That autumn the emperor died; You was enthroned as Emperor An.

sentences.zh: 太后使中黃門送耿姬歸國。

translations.literal: The empress dowager sent a Yellow Gate to escort Lady Geng back to the princedom.
translations.idiomatic: She then sent a eunuch of the Yellow Gates to escort Lady Geng back to the fief.

sentences.zh: 注[一]襁以繒帛為之，即今之小兒繃也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The swaddling is made of silk; it is what today is called the child's binding.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Swaddling was silk cloth, the Han equivalent of an infant's bands.

sentences.zh: 繃音必衡反。

translations.literal: Beng has the sound pi heng fan.
translations.idiomatic: Beng is read pi-heng.

sentences.zh: 帝所生母左姬，字小娥，小娥姊字大娥，犍為人也。

translations.literal: The emperor's birth mother was the Honored Lady Zuo Ji, styled Xiao'e; Xiao'e's elder sister was styled Da'e; they were natives of Qianwei.
translations.idiomatic: His birth mother was the Honored Lady Zuo Ji, called Little E; her elder sister was called Great E, both from Qianwei commandery.

sentences.zh: 初，伯父聖坐妖言伏誅，家屬沒官，二娥數歲入掖庭，及長，並有才色。

translations.literal: At first their uncle Sheng sat guilty of occult talk and was executed; family dependents were confiscated to official service; the two E's at several years of age entered the Rear Palace; when grown, both had talent and beauty.
translations.idiomatic: Their uncle Sheng had been executed for sorcery; the household was confiscated, and the sisters entered the harem as girls. When they came of age, both were gifted and beautiful.

sentences.zh: 小娥善史書，喜辭賦。

translations.literal: Xiao'e was good at clerical script and delighted in rhapsodies.
translations.idiomatic: Little E excelled at clerical script and loved poetic exposition.

sentences.zh: 和帝賜諸王宮人，因入清河第。

translations.literal: Emperor He bestowed palace women on the various princes; thereby she entered the Qinghe residence.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor He assigned palace ladies to the princes, and she entered Prince Qing's household.

sentences.zh: 慶初聞其美，賞傅母以求之。

translations.literal: When Qing first heard of her beauty, he rewarded the nurse to seek her.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Qing, hearing of her loveliness, tipped his nurse to win her for him.

sentences.zh: 及後幸愛極盛，姬妾莫比。

translations.literal: Afterward favor and love were utmost; no concubine could compare.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward his passion for her surpassed every other consort.

sentences.zh: 姊妹皆卒，葬於京師。

translations.literal: The sisters both died and were buried in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Both sisters died and were buried at the capital.

sentences.zh: 注[一]埤音婢。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Pi has the sound bi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Pi is read like bi.

sentences.zh: 注[二]旗有九旒，天子制也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The banner had nine streamers; it was the Son of Heaven's regulation.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Nine tassels on the banner were reserved for the Son of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 恭王強葬，贈以殊禮，升龍、旄頭、鸞輅、龍旗，虎賁百人。

translations.literal: At Prince Gong Qiang's burial, extraordinary rites were conferred: ascending dragon, yak-tail plumes, phoenix carriage, dragon banner, one hundred tiger guards.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Gong Qiang's funeral was granted exceptional honors: the ascending-dragon motif, yak-tail pennons, the phoenix hearse, the dragon banner, and a hundred Tiger Guards.

sentences.zh: 虎威立三年薨，亦無子。

translations.literal: Huwei had been established three years and died; likewise he had no son.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Huwei reigned three years and died, also without an heir.

sentences.zh: 鄧太后復立樂安王寵子延平為清河王，是為恭王。

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Deng again established Yan's son Yanping of Le'an as Prince of Qinghe; this is Prince Gong.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Deng then named Yanping, son of Prince Chong of Le'an, Prince of Qinghe—posthumously Prince Gong.

translations.literal: [One]
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: (footnote marker in the received text)

sentences.zh: 注[一]寵即千乘王伉之子。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Chong is the son of Prince Kang of Qiansheng Kang.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Chong was the son of Liu Kang, Prince Zhen of Qiansheng.

sentences.zh: 太后崩，有司上言：「清河孝王至德淳懿，載育明聖，承天奉祚，為郊廟主。

translations.literal: After the empress dowager died, the responsible officials submitted: 'The Prince Xiao of Qinghe, of utmost virtue and pure excellence, bore and reared the enlightened sage; he received Heaven and held the mandate; he is master of suburban and temple sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: After her death officials memorialized: 'Prince Xiao of Qinghe combined supreme virtue with pure conduct; he fathered the sage-king, received Heaven's charge, and stands as the source of the dynastic line.'

sentences.zh: 漢興，高皇帝尊父為太上皇，宣帝號父為皇考，[一]序昭穆，置園邑。

translations.literal: Since Han arose, the High Emperor honored his father as Grand Supreme Emperor; Emperor Xuandi titled his father Imperial Father [one]; he ordered zhao and mu and established parks and towns.
translations.idiomatic: Since Han's founding, Gaozu exalted his father as Grand Supreme Emperor, and Xuandi gave his father the title Imperial Father, Note [1]: set the temple generations in zhao-mu order, and founded tomb parks.

translations.literal: Manuscript asterisk (linking gloss across the break).
translations.idiomatic: A marginal asterisk in the received text links this gloss across the break.

translations.literal: (tai)
translations.idiomatic: (the character tai, 'grand')

sentences.zh: **[大]*宗之義，舊章不忘。

translations.literal: **[grand]* lineage's meaning: old statutes are not forgotten.
translations.idiomatic: That is the doctrine of the Grand Lineage—ancient precedent must never be forgotten.'

sentences.zh: [二]宜上尊號曰孝德皇，皇妣左氏曰孝德後，孝德皇母宋貴人追謚曰敬隱後。

translations.literal: [Two] It is fitting to raise the honorific title Filial Virtuous August Lord; the imperial mother Lady Zuo as Empress Filial Virtuous; the Filial Virtuous August Lord's mother, the Honored Lady Song, posthumously titled Respectful Hidden Empress.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Let him be canonized as Emperor Xiaode, his mother Lady Zuo as Empress Xiaode, and his grandmother the Honored Lady Song posthumously as Empress Jingyin.'

sentences.zh: 」乃告祠高廟，使司徒持節與大鴻臚奉策書璽綬*[之]*清河，追上尊號；

translations.literal: 'Thereupon they announced sacrifice at Gaozu's temple; they caused the minister of education bearing credentials together with the grand herald to take the patent, document, and seal-ribbons *[to]* Qinghe and raise the honorific titles;
translations.idiomatic: They reported to Gaozu's shrine and sent the minister of education with the grand herald to Qinghe with patents and seals to confer the new titles;

sentences.zh: 又遣中常侍奉太牢祠典，護禮儀侍中劉珍等及宗室列侯皆往會事。

translations.literal: moreover they dispatched a palace attendant to offer the grand bull according to the rites; Director of Palace Gentlemen Liu Zhen and others together with imperial clansmen and ranked marquises all went to oversee the affair.
translations.idiomatic: a eunuch was sent with a great ox for sacrifice, while Liu Zhen and other chamberlains, with nobles of the blood, attended the ceremony.

sentences.zh: 尊陵曰甘陵，廟曰昭廟，置令、丞，設兵車周□，比章陵。

translations.literal: The mausoleum was titled Ganling, the temple Zhaomiao; a director and assistant were appointed; chariots of war were posted in a ring [lacuna], comparable to Zhangling.
translations.idiomatic: His tomb was named Ganling and his temple Zhaomiao, with a custodian and deputy, an armed guard cordon like that at Zhangling.

sentences.zh: [三]復以廣川益清河國。

translations.literal: [Three] Again they enlarged the Principality of Qinghe with Guangchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Guangchuan commandery was added to the princedom.'

sentences.zh: 尊耿姬為甘陵大貴人。

translations.literal: They honored Lady Geng as grand honored lady of Ganling.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Geng was titled grand honored lady of Ganling.

sentences.zh: 又封女弟侍男為涅陽長公主，別得為舞陰長公主，久長為濮陽長公主，直得為平氏長公主。

translations.literal: They also enfeoffed his younger sisters Shinan as senior princess of Nieyang, Biede as senior princess of Wuyin, Jiuchang as senior princess of Puyang, and Zhide as senior princess of Pingshi.
translations.idiomatic: His sisters Shinan, Biede, Jiuchang, and Zhide were made senior princesses of Nieyang, Wuyin, Puyang, and Pingshi respectively.

sentences.zh: 余七主並早卒，故不及進爵。

translations.literal: The remaining seven princesses all died early, therefore they did not advance in rank.
translations.idiomatic: Seven other daughters had died young and received no posthumous advancement.

sentences.zh: 追贈敬隱後女弟小貴人印綬，追封謚宋楊為當陽穆侯。

translations.literal: They posthumously bestowed seal-ribbons on the younger sister of Empress Jingyin, the lesser honored lady; posthumously they enfeoffed and titled Song Yang as marquis Mu of Dangyang.
translations.idiomatic: The Honored Lady's younger sister was given posthumous seals, and Song Yang was posthumously enfeoffed as Marquis Mu of Dangyang.

sentences.zh: [四]楊四子皆為列侯，食邑各五千戶。

translations.literal: [Four] Yang's four sons all became ranked marquises, each eating five thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Each of Yang's four sons became a full marquis with a fief of five thousand households.'

sentences.zh: 宋氏為卿、校、侍中、大夫、謁者、郎吏十餘人。

translations.literal: More than ten men of the Song clan became ministers, colonels, palace attendants, grandees, ushers, and gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Over a dozen Song kinsmen received appointments as ministers, colonels, attendants, grandees, ushers, and gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 孝德後異母弟次及達生二人，諸子九人，皆為清河國郎中。

translations.literal: The Filial Virtuous Empress's half-brothers Ci and Dasheng, two men, and nine sons—all became gentlemen in the palace of Qinghe.
translations.idiomatic: Her half-brothers Ci and Dasheng, with nine sons, were all named gentlemen of the Qinghe household.

sentences.zh: 耿貴人者，牟平侯舒之孫也。

translations.literal: The Honored Lady Geng was a granddaughter of Marquis Mouping Shu.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Geng was a granddaughter of Marquis Mouping Geng Shu.

sentences.zh: 貴人兄寶，襲封牟平侯。

translations.literal: The honored lady's elder brother Bao inherited the fief of Marquis Mouping.
translations.idiomatic: Her brother Bao inherited the title Marquis of Mouping.

sentences.zh: 帝以寶嫡舅，寵遇甚渥，位至大將軍，事已見耿舒傳。

translations.literal: The emperor, because Bao was the principal maternal uncle, favor and encounter were very thick; his position reached grand general; the affair is already seen in Geng Shu's biography.
translations.idiomatic: As the emperor's chief uncle on the mother's side, Bao enjoyed lavish favor and rose to grand general; the rest is told in Geng Shu's biography.

sentences.zh: 注[一]宣帝父諱進，武帝時號史皇孫，坐戾太子事遇害。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Xuandi's father's taboo name was Jin; in Emperor Wu's time he was titled Imperial Grandson of History; he sat the affair of the heir Li and met harm.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Xuandi's father bore the taboo name Jin; under Wu he was known as the imperial grandson of the heir apparent and perished in the Li heir's case.

sentences.zh: 帝即位，追尊皇考，立廟。

translations.literal: When the emperor took the throne, he posthumously honored the imperial father and established a temple.
translations.idiomatic: On his accession Xuandi canonized his father and founded a temple to him.

sentences.zh: 注[二]*

translations.literal: Commentary [two]*
translations.idiomatic: Second commentary note; the asterisk marks that the gloss on the Grand Lineage continues on the next line.

translations.literal: (Grand)
translations.idiomatic: (Tai)

sentences.zh: **[大]*宗謂繼嗣也。

translations.literal: **[Greatest]* lineage means succession.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Lineage here means legitimate succession.

sentences.zh: 左傳季桓子曰「舊章不可忘」也。

translations.literal: Ji Huanzi of the Zuo Zhuan said 'old statutes cannot be forgotten.'
translations.idiomatic: As Ji Huanzi says in the Zuo zhuan, 'Ancient precedent must not be cast aside.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]皇考南頓君陵。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The imperial father's tomb at Nandun.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: The imperial father's tomb was at Nandun.

sentences.zh: 注[四]當陽，今荊州也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Dangyang is in modern Jingzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: Dangyang lay in what is now Jingzhou.

sentences.zh: [延平]*立三十五年薨，子蒜嗣。

translations.literal: [Yanping]* having been established thirty-five years died; his son Suan succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Yanping reigned thirty-five years and died; his son Liu Suan inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 珍帝崩，征蒜詣京師，將議為嗣。

translations.literal: When Emperor Zhi died, Suan was summoned to the capital; they were about to deliberate making him successor.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Zhi died, Liu Suan was called to Luoyang as a candidate for the throne.

sentences.zh: 會大將軍梁冀與梁太后立質帝，罷歸國。

translations.literal: It happened that Grand General Liang Ji with Empress Dowager Liang established Emperor Zhi; he was dismissed and returned to the state.
translations.idiomatic: Grand General Liang Ji and Empress Dowager Liang enthroned Emperor Zhi instead, and Suan was sent back to his fief.

sentences.zh: 蒜為人嚴重，動止有度，朝臣太尉李固等莫不歸心焉。

translations.literal: Suan as a person was stern and weighty; in movement and rest he had measure; court ministers such as Grand Commandant Li Gu had none who did not turn their hearts to him.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Suan was grave and dignified, measured in every gesture; ministers such as Li Gu looked to him as their choice.

sentences.zh: 初，中常侍曹騰謁蒜，蒜不為禮，宦者由此惡之。

translations.literal: At first Palace Attendant Cao Teng visited Suan; Suan did not perform courtesy; by this the eunuchs hated him.
translations.idiomatic: When the eunuch Cao Teng paid his respects, Suan snubbed him, and the palace attendants never forgave it.

sentences.zh: 及帝崩，公卿皆正議立蒜，而曹騰說梁冀不聽，遂立桓帝。

translations.literal: When the emperor died, the dukes and ministers all debated uprightly to establish Suan, but Cao Teng persuaded Liang Ji not to listen; thereupon they established Emperor Huan.
translations.idiomatic: At the next vacancy the high ministers unanimously favored Suan, but Cao Teng talked Liang Ji out of it, and Emperor Huan was chosen instead.

sentences.zh: 語在李固傳。

translations.literal: The words are in Li Gu's biography.
translations.idiomatic: The details appear in Li Gu's biography.

sentences.zh: 蒜由此得罪。

translations.literal: Suan by this incurred guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Suan thus fell under suspicion.

sentences.zh: 建和元年，甘陵人劉文與南郡妖賊劉鮪交通，訛言清河王當統天子，欲共立蒜。

translations.literal: In the first year of Jianhe, a man of Ganling named Liu Wen communicated with the sorcerer-rebel Liu Wei of Nan commandery; false words said the Prince of Qinghe ought to rule as Son of Heaven; they wished together to establish Suan.
translations.idiomatic: In 147 CE a Ganling man, Liu Wen, conspired with the rebel Liu Wei of Nan, spreading word that Heaven destined the Prince of Qinghe for the throne and plotting to set Liu Suan on it.

sentences.zh: 事發覺，文等遂劫清河相謝暠，將至王宮司馬門，[一]曰：「當立王為天子，暠為公。

translations.literal: When the affair was discovered, Wen and others thereupon seized the chancellor of Qinghe, Xie Hao, and brought him to the prince's palace gate of the marshal, [one] saying: 'We ought to establish the king as Son of Heaven, Hao as duke.'
translations.idiomatic: When the plot broke, Wen's band seized Chancellor Xie Hao of Qinghe and hauled him to the princely gate, Note [1]: shouting that the prince must be made emperor and Xie Hao grand duke.'

sentences.zh: 」暠不聽，罵之，文因刺殺暠。

translations.literal: 'Hao would not listen; he reviled them; Wen therefore stabbed and killed Hao.
translations.idiomatic: Xie Hao refused and cursed them, so Wen killed him.'

sentences.zh: 於是捕文、鮪誅之。

translations.literal: Thereupon they seized Wen and Wei and executed them.
translations.idiomatic: Wen and Wei were captured and executed.

sentences.zh: 有司因劾奏蒜，坐貶爵為尉氏侯，徙桂陽，自殺。

translations.literal: The responsible officials thereupon impeached and memorialized Suan; he sat guilt and was demoted in rank to Marquis of Weishi, transferred to Guiyang, and killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: The authorities impeached Liu Suan; he was degraded to Marquis of Weishi, banished to Guiyang, and committed suicide.

sentences.zh: 立三年，國絕。

translations.literal: Having been established three years, the state was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: After three years the line was extinguished.

sentences.zh: 注[一]帝紀「謝」作「射」，蓋紀傳不同。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The annals write Xie as She; probably annals and biography differ.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The basic annals read the surname as She—scribal variants between annals and biography.

sentences.zh: 梁冀惡清河名，明年，乃改為甘陵。

translations.literal: Liang Ji hated the name Qinghe; the next year therefore it was changed to Ganling.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji loathed the name Qinghe, so the next year the princedom was renamed Ganling.

sentences.zh: 梁太后立安平孝王子經侯理為甘陵王，[一] 奉孝德皇祀，是為威王。

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Liang established Li, marquis of Jing of Prince Xiao of Ping'an's son, as Prince of Ganling, [one] to continue the sacrifices to Emperor Xiaode; this is Prince Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Liang named Li, marquis of Jing and son of the Prince of Ping'an, Prince of Ganling, Note [1]: to maintain the cult of Emperor Xiaode—posthumously Prince Wei.

sentences.zh: 注[一]安平王德，河閒王開子。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Prince De of Ping'an was a son of Prince Kai of Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Prince De of Ping'an was a son of Liu Kai, Prince of Hejian.

sentences.zh: 理立二十五年薨，子貞王定嗣。

translations.literal: Li having been established twenty-five years died; his son Prince Zhen Ding succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Li reigned twenty-five years; his son Ding, Prince Zhen, succeeded.

sentences.zh: 定立四年薨，子獻王忠嗣。

translations.literal: Ding having been established four years died; his son Prince Xian Zhong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Ding reigned four years; his son Zhong, Prince Xian, followed.

sentences.zh: 黃巾賊起，忠為國人所執，既而釋之。

translations.literal: When the Yellow Turban rebels rose, Zhong was seized by the people of the state; afterward they released him.
translations.idiomatic: When the Yellow Turbans rose, the locals seized Zhong but later let him go.

sentences.zh: 靈帝以親親故，詔復忠國。

translations.literal: Emperor Ling, because of closeness of kin, edicted restoring Zhong's state.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ling, honoring the blood tie, issued an edict restoring the princedom.

sentences.zh: 忠立十三年薨，嗣子為黃巾所害，建安十一年，以無後，國除。

translations.literal: Zhong having been established thirteen years died; the heir son was harmed by the Yellow Turbans; in the eleventh year of Jian'an, because there was no posterity, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong reigned thirteen years; his heir fell victim to the rebels, and in 206 CE, the eleventh year of Jian'an, the fief ended for lack of successors.

sentences.zh: 濟北惠王壽

translations.literal: Prince Hui of Jibei, Shou.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Hui of Jibei, Liu Shou.

sentences.zh: 濟北惠王壽，母申貴人，穎川人也，世吏二千石。

translations.literal: Prince Hui of Jibei, Shou—his mother was the Honored Lady Shen, a native of Yingchuan; for generations they had been officials at two thousand bushels.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Shou's mother was the Honored Lady Shen of Yingchuan, from a family of long-standing two-thousand-bushel officials.

sentences.zh: 貴人年十三，入掖庭。

translations.literal: The honored lady at thirteen years entered the Rear Palace.
translations.idiomatic: She entered the harem at thirteen.

sentences.zh: 壽以永元二年封，分太山郡為國。

translations.literal: Shou in the second year of Yongyuan was enfeoffed; Tai commandery was divided to make the state.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Shou was enfeoffed in 90 CE, the second year of Yongyuan, with territory carved from Taishan commandery.

sentences.zh: 和帝遵肅宗故事，兄弟皆留京師，恩寵篤密。

translations.literal: Emperor He followed Emperor Zhang's precedent; the brothers all remained in the capital; grace and favor were thick and close.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor He, following Zhang's example, kept his brothers in the capital under lavish favor.

sentences.zh: 有司請遣諸王歸藩，不忍許之，及帝崩，乃就國。

translations.literal: The responsible officials requested dispatching the various princes to their fan domains; he could not bear to permit it; when the emperor died, only then did they go to their states.
translations.idiomatic: Officials repeatedly asked to send the princes to their fiefs, but he always refused; not until his death did they depart.

sentences.zh: 永初元年，鄧太后封壽舅申轉為新亭侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongchu, Empress Dowager Deng enfeoffed Shou's maternal uncle Shen Zhuan as marquis of Xinting.
translations.idiomatic: In 107 CE Empress Dowager Deng made Shou's uncle Shen Zhuan Marquis of Xinting.

sentences.zh: 壽立三十一年薨。

translations.literal: Shou having been established thirty-one years died.
translations.idiomatic: Shou reigned thirty-one years, then died.

sentences.zh: 自永初已後，戎狄叛亂，國用不足，始封王薨，減賻錢為千萬，布萬匹；

translations.literal: From Yongchu onward, Rong and Di rebelled and the state was insufficient in use; from the time the first enfeoffed kings died, burial gifts were reduced to ten million cash and ten thousand bolts of cloth;
translations.idiomatic: After Yongchu, barbarian revolts strained the treasury, so when a first-generation prince died his funeral subvention was cut to ten million cash and ten thousand bolts of cloth;

sentences.zh: 嗣王薨，五百萬，布五千匹。

translations.literal: When a successor king died, five million cash, five thousand bolts of cloth.
translations.idiomatic: For a second-generation prince's funeral the grant was five million cash and five thousand bolts of cloth.

sentences.zh: 時唯壽最尊親，特賻錢三千萬，布三萬匹。

translations.literal: At that time only Shou was most honored in kinship; specially burial gifts were three million cash, thirty thousand bolts of cloth.
translations.idiomatic: Shou alone, as the emperor's closest kinsman among the princes, received three million cash and thirty thousand bolts for his obsequies.

sentences.zh: 子節王登嗣。

translations.literal: His son, Prince Jie, Deng succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Deng, Prince Jie, inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 永寧元年，封登弟五人為鄉侯，皆別食太山邑。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongning, five younger brothers of Deng were enfeoffed as village marquises, each separately eating towns in Tai commandery.
translations.idiomatic: In 120 CE Deng's five younger brothers were made village marquises with separate incomes from towns in Taishan.

sentences.zh: 登立十五年薨，子哀王多嗣。

translations.literal: Deng having been established fifteen years died; his son, Prince Ai, Duo succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Deng reigned fifteen years; his son Duo, Prince Ai, followed.

sentences.zh: 多立三年薨，無子。

translations.literal: Duo having been established three years died; he had no son.
translations.idiomatic: Duo died after three years without an heir.

sentences.zh: 永和四年，立戰鄉侯安國為濟北王，是為厘王。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Yonghe, Marquis of Zhanxiang Anguo was established as Prince of Jibei; this is Prince Li.
translations.idiomatic: In 139 CE Marquis Anguo of Zhanxiang was named Prince of Jibei—posthumously Prince Li (Xi).

translations.literal: [One]
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: (footnote marker)

sentences.zh: 注[一]厘音僖也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Li is read like xi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The title Li is read like xi (the auspicious graph).

sentences.zh: 安國立*

translations.literal: Anguo having been established*
translations.idiomatic: Anguo reigned

translations.literal: (ten)
translations.idiomatic: (ten)

sentences.zh: **[七]*年薨，子孝王次嗣。

translations.literal: **[seven]* years died; his son, Prince Xiao, Ci succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: seventeen years, then died; his son Ci, Prince Xiao, succeeded.

sentences.zh: 本初元年，封次弟猛為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Benchu, Ci's younger brother Meng was enfeoffed as village marquis.
translations.idiomatic: In 146 CE his brother Meng received a village marquisate.

sentences.zh: 次九歲喪父，至孝。

translations.literal: Ci at nine years lost his father; he was supremely filial.
translations.idiomatic: Ci lost his father at nine and showed extraordinary filial piety.

sentences.zh: 建和元年，梁太后下詔曰：「濟北王次以幼年守藩，躬履孝道，父沒哀慟，焦毀過禮，草廬土席，衰杖在身，頭不枇沐，體生瘡腫。

translations.literal: In the first year of Jianhe, Empress Dowager Liang issued an edict saying: 'The Prince of Jibei, Ci, in childhood guards the fan domain, personally practices filial way; when his father died he mourned in grief, withered and ruined beyond the rites; grass hut and earthen mat, mourning staff on his person, his head not combed or washed, his body produced sores and swellings.'
translations.idiomatic: In 147 CE Empress Dowager Liang proclaimed: 'Prince Ci of Jibei, though a child at his post, has lived the full mourning rites for his father—emaciated past what ritual demands, dwelling in a thatched hut on bare earth, staff in hand, forgoing comb and bath until his skin festered.'

sentences.zh: 諒闇已來二十八月，自諸國有憂，未之聞也，朝廷甚嘉焉。

translations.literal: From the concealed mourning already twenty-eight months; from the time the various states had grief, it has not been heard of; the court greatly praised it.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty-eight months of vigil have passed; no other prince's domain has shown the like, and the court acclaims him.'

sentences.zh: 書不雲乎：『用德章厥善。

translations.literal: Does not the Document say: "Use virtue to make manifest their good."
translations.idiomatic: The Documents say, "Make goodness shine forth through virtue."'

sentences.zh: 』[一] 詩云：『孝子不匱，永錫爾類。

translations.literal: '[One] The Classic of Poetry says: 'The filial son is not exhausted; forever he bestows on you your kind.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: And the Odes: 'The dutiful son knows no end; Heaven long enriches your house.'"'

sentences.zh: 』[二]今增次封五千戶，廣其土宇，以慰孝子惻隱之勞。」

translations.literal: '[Two] Now increase Ci's enfeoffment by five thousand households, widen his territory and soil, thereby to comfort the filial son's compassionate toil.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Add five thousand households to his fief and broaden his lands, to reward such filial devotion.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一]尚書盤庚之辭也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: It is a phrase from the Documents, Pan Geng.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: A line from the "Pan Geng" chapter of the Shang shu.

sentences.zh: 言以道德明之，使競為善也。

translations.literal: It means by virtue and the Way to make it clear, causing them to compete in doing good.
translations.idiomatic: It means to illuminate goodness through moral example so that all strive toward virtue.

sentences.zh: 注[二]詩大雅也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: It is from the Classic of Poetry, Greater Odes.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: From the Greater Odes of the Classic of Poetry.

sentences.zh: 匱，竭也。

translations.literal: Kui means exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Kui means 'exhausted' or 'spent.'

sentences.zh: 類，善也。

translations.literal: Lei means good.
translations.idiomatic: Lei means 'kind' or 'worthy line.'

sentences.zh: 永，長也。

translations.literal: Yong means long.
translations.idiomatic: Yong means 'lasting.'

sentences.zh: 言孝子之行，無有匱竭，長賜與汝之族類，教道天下。

translations.literal: It means the filial son's conduct has nothing exhausted; long he bestows on you your kind, instructing and guiding All under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The line praises a son whose devotion never flags and whose virtue Heaven perpetuates in his kin, a model to the realm.

sentences.zh: 次立*[十]*七年薨，子鸞嗣。

translations.literal: Ci having been established *[ten]* seventeen years died; his son Luan succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Ci reigned seventeen years and died; his son Liu Luan succeeded.

sentences.zh: 鸞薨，子政嗣。

translations.literal: Luan died; his son Zheng succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Luan died and was succeeded by Zheng.

sentences.zh: 政薨，無子，建安十一年，國除。

translations.literal: Zheng died; he had no son; in the eleventh year of Jian'an, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng left no son, and in 206 CE the princedom was struck off.

sentences.zh: 河閒孝王開

translations.literal: Prince Xiao of Hejian, Kai.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Xiao of Hejian, Liu Kai.

sentences.zh: 河閒孝王開，以永元二年封，分樂成、勃海、涿郡為國。

translations.literal: Prince Xiao of Hejian, Kai—in the second year of Yongyuan was enfeoffed; Lecheng, Bohai, and Zhuo commanderies were divided to make the state.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Kai was enfeoffed in 90 CE, the second year of Yongyuan, with territory taken from Lecheng, Bohai, and Zhuo.

sentences.zh: 延平元年就國。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yanping he went to his state.
translations.idiomatic: He took up his fief in 106 CE, the first year of Yanping.

sentences.zh: 開奉遵法度，吏人敬之。

translations.literal: Kai upheld and observed the laws and measures; clerks and people revered him.
translations.idiomatic: Kai obeyed the law to the letter and won the respect of officials and commoners alike.

sentences.zh: 永寧元年，鄧太后封開子翼為平原王，奉懷王勝祀；

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongning, Empress Dowager Deng enfeoffed Kai's son Yi as Prince of Pingyuan, to continue the sacrifices to Prince Huai Sheng;
translations.idiomatic: In 120 CE Empress Dowager Deng named Liu Yi, Kai's son, Prince of Pingyuan to maintain the ancestral cult of Prince Huai Liu Sheng;

sentences.zh: [一] 子德為安平王，奉樂成王黨祀。

translations.literal: [One] his son De was Prince of Ping'an, continuing the sacrifices to Prince of Lecheng Dang.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: his son De became Prince of Ping'an, heir to the line of Prince Dang of Lecheng.

translations.literal: [Two]
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: (marker)

sentences.zh: 注[一]勝，和帝子。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Sheng was a son of Emperor He.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Liu Sheng was Emperor He's son.

sentences.zh: 注[二]黨，明帝子也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Dang was a son of Emperor Ming.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Liu Dang was Emperor Ming's son.

sentences.zh: 開立四十二年薨，子惠王政嗣。

translations.literal: Kai having been established forty-two years died; his son, Prince Hui, Zheng succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Kai reigned forty-two years; Liu Zheng, Prince Hui, inherited.

sentences.zh: 政□佷，不奉法憲。

translations.literal: Zheng [lacuna] was stubborn and cruel; he did not uphold the statutes.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng was truculent and lawless—lacuna in the text for one graph.

sentences.zh: 順帝以侍御史吳郡沈景有強能稱，故擢為河閒相。

translations.literal: Emperor Shun, because Attending Secretary Shen Jing of Wu commandery had a reputation for strength and ability, therefore promoted him to be chancellor of Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Shun named Shen Jing of Wu, an attending secretary known for toughness and competence, chancellor of Hejian.

sentences.zh: 景到國謁王，王不正服，箕踞殿上。

translations.literal: When Jing arrived in the state and visited the prince, the prince did not correct his dress and sat with legs spread on the hall.
translations.idiomatic: At his first audience Zheng wore no proper court dress and lolled on the dais with legs sprawled.

sentences.zh: 侍郎贊拜，景峙不為禮。

translations.literal: The gentleman attendant announced the bow; Jing stood upright and did not perform courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: When the usher called for obeisance, Jing stood stiffly and made no bow.

sentences.zh: [一]問王所在，虎賁曰：「是非王邪？

translations.literal: [One] He asked where the prince was; a tiger guard said: 'Is not this the prince?'
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Jing demanded where the prince was; a Tiger Guard replied, 'Is this not he?'

sentences.zh: 」景曰：「王不服，常人何別！

translations.literal: 'Jing said: 'If the prince will not dress correctly, how is he different from an ordinary man!'
translations.idiomatic: Jing retorted, 'Without princely dress he is no different from a commoner!'

sentences.zh: 今相謁王，豈謁無禮者邪！

translations.literal: 'Now the chancellor visits the prince—can it be that he visits one without courtesy!'
translations.idiomatic: I came to greet a prince, not an ill-mannered lout!'

sentences.zh: 」王籩而更服，景然後拜。

translations.literal: 'The prince thereupon changed dress; only then did Jing bow.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng scrambled into proper robes; only then did Jing salute.

sentences.zh: 出住宮門外，請王傅責之曰：「前發京師，陛下見受詔，以王不恭，使檢督。

translations.literal: He went out and lodged outside the palace gate, summoned the prince's tutor and reproached him saying: 'Previously when departing the capital, His Majesty received me with an edict because the prince was disrespectful and ordered inspection and supervision.'
translations.idiomatic: Outside the gate he summoned the tutors and rebuked them: 'Before I left Luoyang the emperor charged me, citing the prince's rudeness, to set matters right.'

sentences.zh: 諸君空受爵祿，而無訓導之義。

translations.literal: You gentlemen vainly receive rank and stipend yet have no duty of instruction and guidance.'
translations.idiomatic: You draw salaries but have failed to instruct him.'

sentences.zh: 」因奏治罪。

translations.literal: 'Thereupon he memorialized to punish crime.
translations.idiomatic: He then filed charges.

sentences.zh: 詔書讓政而詰責傅。

translations.literal: An edict reproached Zheng and interrogated the tutors.
translations.idiomatic: The throne rebuked Zheng and called the tutors to account.

sentences.zh: 景因捕諸奸人上案其罪，[二]殺戮尤惡者數十人，出冤獄百餘人。

translations.literal: Jing thereupon seized various evil men and submitted upward their crimes, [two] killed and executed several tens who were especially vile, and released more than a hundred from unjust prisons.
translations.idiomatic: Jing rounded up the worst offenders, Note [2]: executed dozens of hardened criminals, and freed over a hundred wrongly jailed innocents.

sentences.zh: 政遂為改節，悔過自修。

translations.literal: Zheng thereupon changed his conduct, repented errors, and cultivated himself.
translations.idiomatic: Chastened, Zheng mended his ways.

sentences.zh: 陽嘉元年，封政弟十三人皆為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yangjia, thirteen younger brothers of Zheng were all enfeoffed as village marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In 132 CE thirteen of his brothers received village marquisates.

sentences.zh: 注[一]峙，立也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Zhi means to stand.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Zhi means 'to stand stiffly.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]上，奏上也，音市丈反。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Shang means to submit upward; the sound is shi zhang fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Shang here means 'to memorialize'; read shi-zhang (上聲).

sentences.zh: 政立十年薨，子貞王建嗣。

translations.literal: Zheng having been established ten years died; his son, Prince Zhen, Jian succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng reigned ten years; Jian, Prince Zhen, succeeded.

sentences.zh: 建立十年薨，子安王利嗣。

translations.literal: Jian having been established ten years died; his son, Prince An, Li succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Jian reigned ten years; Liu Li, Prince An, followed.

sentences.zh: 利立二十八年薨，子陔嗣。

translations.literal: Li having been established twenty-eight years died; his son Gai succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Li reigned twenty-eight years; his son Gai succeeded.

sentences.zh: 陔立四十一年，魏受禪，以為崇德侯。

translations.literal: Gai having been established forty-one years, Wei received the abdication; he was made Marquis of Chongde.
translations.idiomatic: After forty-one years under Gai, Wei accepted the Han abdication and named him Marquis of Chongde.

sentences.zh: 蠡吾侯翼，元初六年鄧太后征濟北、河閒王諸子詣京師，奇翼美儀容，故以為平原懷王后焉。

translations.literal: Marquis of Liyi, Yi—in the sixth year of Yuanchu Empress Dowager Deng summoned the sons of the princes of Jibei and Hejian to the capital; she marveled at Yi's fine bearing and therefore made him successor to Prince Huai of Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: In 119 CE Empress Dowager Deng called the young princes of Jibei and Hejian to court, was struck by Liu Yi's presence, and named him heir to Prince Huai of Pingyuan.

sentences.zh: [一]留在京師。

translations.literal: [One] He was retained in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: He remained in Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，太后崩。

translations.literal: After more than a year the empress dowager died.
translations.idiomatic: A year later the empress dowager died.

sentences.zh: 安帝乳母王聖與中常侍江京等譖鄧騭兄弟及翼，雲與中大夫趙王謀圖不軌，窺覦神器，懷大逆心。

translations.literal: Emperor An's wet nurse Wang Sheng with Palace Attendant Jiang Jing and others slandered the Deng Zhi brothers and Yi, saying they plotted with Palace Grandee Wang of Zhao for unorthodox ends, coveting the sacred vessel, harboring a heart of great rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Sheng, the emperor's nurse, and Jiang Jing accused Deng Zhi's clan and Liu Yi of conspiring with Grandee Wang of Zhao to seize the throne.

sentences.zh: [二]貶為都鄉侯，遣歸河閒。

translations.literal: [Two] He was demoted to Marquis of Duxiang and sent back to Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Yi was cut to Marquis of Duxiang and returned to Hejian.

sentences.zh: 翼於是謝賓客，閉門自處。

translations.literal: Yi thereupon dismissed guests and closed his gate to dwell alone.
translations.idiomatic: Yi shut his doors and dismissed his clients.

sentences.zh: 永建五年，父開上書，願分蠡吾縣以封翼，順帝從之。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yongjian, his father Kai submitted a memorial, wishing to divide Liyi county to enfeoff Yi; Emperor Shun followed it.
translations.idiomatic: In 130 CE his father Kai asked to split off Liyi county for Yi's fief, and Shun agreed.

sentences.zh: 注[一]平原王得無子，故立之也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Prince of Pingyuan happened to have no son; therefore he was established.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Pingyuan lacked an heir, so Yi was chosen.

sentences.zh: 注[二]神器喻帝位也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The sacred vessel is a metaphor for the imperial seat.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: 'Sacred vessel' is a stock phrase for the throne.

sentences.zh: 老子曰：「天下神器，不可為也。」

translations.literal: Laozi states: 'All under Heaven's sacred vessel cannot be acted on.'
translations.idiomatic: The Laozi says, 'The empire is a sacred trust; it cannot be seized by force.'

sentences.zh: 翼卒，子志嗣，為大將軍梁冀所立，是為桓帝。

translations.literal: Yi died; his son Zhi succeeded; he was established by Grand General Liang Ji; this is Emperor Huan.
translations.idiomatic: Yi died; his son Zhi became Emperor Huan at Liang Ji's instigation.

sentences.zh: 梁太后詔追尊河閒孝王為孝穆皇，夫人趙氏曰孝穆後，廟曰清廟，陵曰樂成陵；

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Liang edicted posthumously honoring Prince Xiao of Hejian as Emperor Xiaomu, Lady Zhao as Empress Xiaomu; the temple was called Qingmiao, the mausoleum Lechengling;
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Liang canonized Liu Kai as Emperor Xiaomu, his consort Lady Zhao as empress, with temple Qing and tomb Lechengling;

sentences.zh: 蠡吾先侯曰孝崇皇，廟曰烈廟，陵曰博陵。

translations.literal: the former Marquis of Liyi as Emperor Xiaochong, temple Liemiao, tomb Boling.
translations.idiomatic: Yi was honored as Emperor Xiaochong with temple Lie and tomb Boling.

sentences.zh: 皆置令、丞，使司徒持節奉策書、璽綬，祠以太牢。

translations.literal: In all cases directors and assistants were appointed; she caused the minister of education bearing credentials to take the patent, document, and seal-ribbons and sacrifice with the grand bull.
translations.idiomatic: Custodians were named for each site, and the minister of education brought patents, seals, and a great ox for sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 建和二年，更封帝*

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianhe, she transferred the enfeoffment: the emperor's
translations.idiomatic: In 148 CE the title was reassigned: the emperor's

translations.literal: (elder brother)
translations.idiomatic: (elder brother)

sentences.zh: **[弟]*都鄉侯碩為平原王，留博陵，奉翼後。

translations.literal: **[younger brother]* Marquis of Duxiang Shuo as Prince of Pingyuan, retaining him at Boling, to continue Yi's posterity.
translations.idiomatic: His younger brother, Marquis Shuo of Duxiang, was made Prince of Pingyuan, kept at Boling to carry on Liu Yi's line.

sentences.zh: 尊翼夫人馬氏為孝崇博園貴人，以涿郡之良鄉、故安，河閒之蠡吾三縣為湯沐邑。

translations.literal: She honored Yi's consort Lady Ma as honored lady of Xiaochong's Bo garden, and made Liangxiang and Gu'an in Zhuo commandery together with Liyi in Hejian three counties his bath towns.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Ma became honored lady of the Bo garden fief, with Liangxiang, Gu'an, and Liyi as her maintenance lands.

sentences.zh: 碩嗜酒，多過失，帝令馬貴人領王家事。

translations.literal: Shuo was fond of wine and had many faults; the emperor ordered Lady Ma to supervise the royal household.
translations.idiomatic: Shuo drank heavily and misbehaved, so the emperor put Lady Ma in charge of the princely household.

sentences.zh: 建安十一年，國除。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year of Jian'an, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: The principality ended in 206 CE.

sentences.zh: 解瀆亭侯淑，以河閒孝王子封。

translations.literal: Marquis of Jiedu Village Shu was enfeoffed as a son of Prince Xiao of Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Shu, Marquis of Jiedu ting, was a son of Prince Xiao of Hejian.

sentences.zh: 淑卒，子*

translations.literal: Shu died; his son*
translations.idiomatic: Shu died; his son

translations.literal: (chang)
translations.idiomatic: (chang)

translations.literal: **[chang]* succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Chang succeeded.

translations.literal: Manuscript asterisk linking the gloss across the break.
translations.idiomatic: The marginal asterisk links this line to the gloss on the preceding name.

translations.literal: (chang)
translations.idiomatic: (chang)

sentences.zh: **[萇]*卒，子宏嗣，為大將軍竇武所立，是為靈帝。

translations.literal: **[chang]* died; his son Hong succeeded; he was established by Grand General Dou Wu; this is Emperor Ling.
translations.idiomatic: Chang died; Hong became Emperor Ling through Dou Wu's coup.

sentences.zh: 建寧元年，竇太后詔追尊皇祖淑為孝元皇，夫人夏氏曰孝元後，陵曰敦陵，廟曰靖廟；

translations.literal: In the first year of Jianning, Empress Dowager Dou edicted posthumously honoring the imperial grandfather Shu as Emperor Xiaoyuan, Lady Xia as Empress Xiaoyuan; the mausoleum was called Dunling, the temple Jingmiao;
translations.idiomatic: In 168 CE Empress Dowager Dou honored Shu as Emperor Xiaoyuan and Lady Xia as empress, with tomb Dun and temple Jing;

sentences.zh: 皇考長為孝仁皇，夫人董氏為慎園貴人，陵曰慎陵，廟曰奐廟。

translations.literal: the imperial father Chang as Emperor Xiaoren, Lady Dong as honored lady of the Shen garden; the mausoleum was called Shenling, the temple Huanmiao.
translations.idiomatic: Hong's father Chang was honored as Emperor Xiaoren, Lady Dong as honored lady of the Shen garden, with tomb Shenling and temple Huanmiao.

sentences.zh: 皆置令、丞，使司徒持節之河閒奉策書、璽綬，祠以太牢，常以歲時遣中常侍持節之河閒奉祠。
