translations.literal: In all cases directors and assistants were appointed; she caused the minister of education bearing credentials to go to Hejian to take the patent, document, and seal-ribbons and sacrifice with the grand bull; constantly at the year's seasons she dispatched a palace attendant bearing credentials to Hejian to perform sacrifice.
translations.idiomatic: Custodians were installed, the minister of education went to Hejian with patents and seals for the grand sacrifice, and eunuchs were sent yearly to offer worship.

sentences.zh: 熹平三年，使使拜河閒安王利子康為濟南王，奉孝仁皇祀。

translations.literal: In the third year of Xiping, she dispatched envoys to invest Kang, son of Prince An Li of Hejian, as Prince of Jinan, to continue the sacrifices to Emperor Xiaoren.
translations.idiomatic: In 174 CE Kang, son of Prince Li of Hejian, was named Prince of Jinan to tend Emperor Xiaoren's cult.

sentences.zh: 康薨，子贇嗣，建安十二年，為黃巾賊所害。

translations.literal: Kang died; his son Yun succeeded; in the twelfth year of Jian'an he was harmed by Yellow Turban rebels.
translations.idiomatic: Kang's heir Yun fell to the Yellow Turbans in 207 CE.

sentences.zh: 子開嗣，立十三年，魏受禪，以為崇德侯。

translations.literal: His son Kai succeeded; having been established thirteen years, Wei received the abdication; he was made Marquis of Chongde.
translations.idiomatic: Kai reigned thirteen years until Wei ended Han and made him Marquis of Chongde.

sentences.zh: 城陽懷王淑

translations.literal: Prince Huai of Chengyang, Shu.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Huai of Chengyang, Liu Shu.

sentences.zh: 城陽懷王淑，以永元二年分濟陰為國。

translations.literal: Prince Huai of Chengyang, Shu—in the second year of Yongyuan Ji Yin was divided to make the state.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Shu was enfeoffed in 90 CE, the second year of Yongyuan, when Jiyin commandery was split to form his principality.

sentences.zh: 立五年薨，葬於京師。

translations.literal: Having been established five years he died and was buried in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: He died after five years and was buried at Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 無子，國除，還並濟陰。

translations.literal: Having no son, the state was abolished and returned to merge with Ji Yin.
translations.idiomatic: With no heir the fief was abolished and its territory reverted to Jiyin.

sentences.zh: 廣宗殤王萬歲

translations.literal: Prince Shang of Guangzong, Wansui.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Shang of Guangzong, Liu Wansui.

sentences.zh: 廣宗殤王萬歲，以永元五年封，分鉅鹿為國。

translations.literal: Prince Shang of Guangzong, Wansui—in the fifth year of Yongyuan was enfeoffed; Julu was divided to make the state.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Wansui received his title in 93 CE, the fifth year of Yongyuan, from land taken from Julu.

sentences.zh: 其年薨，葬於京師。

translations.literal: That year he died and was buried in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: He died the same year and was interred at Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 無子，國除，還並鉅鹿。

translations.literal: Having no son, the state was abolished and returned to merge with Julu.
translations.idiomatic: With no heir the fief was struck off and its lands reverted to Julu commandery.

sentences.zh: 平原懷王勝

translations.literal: Prince Huai of Pingyuan, Sheng.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Huai of Pingyuan, Liu Sheng.

sentences.zh: 平原懷王勝，和帝長子也。

translations.literal: Prince Huai of Pingyuan, Sheng, was Emperor He's eldest son.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Sheng was Emperor He's firstborn.

sentences.zh: 不載母氏。

translations.literal: His mother's clan is not recorded.
translations.idiomatic: The text does not name his mother.

sentences.zh: 少有痼疾，延平元年封。

translations.literal: From youth he had chronic illness; in the first year of Yanping he was enfeoffed.
translations.idiomatic: He suffered a chronic ailment from childhood and received his title in 106 CE, the first year of Yanping.

sentences.zh: 立八年薨，葬於京師。

translations.literal: Having been established eight years he died and was buried in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: He reigned eight years, died, and was buried at Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 無子，鄧太后立樂安夷王寵子得為平原王，奉勝後，是為哀王。

translations.literal: Having no son, Empress Dowager Deng established Chong's son De of Le'an as Prince of Pingyuan, to continue Sheng's posterity; this is Prince Ai.
translations.idiomatic: Childless, he was succeeded by De, son of Prince Yi of Le'an, as Prince of Pingyuan—posthumously Prince Ai—to maintain Liu Sheng's line.

sentences.zh: 得立六年薨，無子，永寧元年，太后又立河閒王開子都鄉侯翼為平原王嗣。

translations.literal: De having been established six years died; having no son, in the first year of Yongning the empress dowager again established Marquis of Duxiang Yi, son of Prince Kai of Hejian, as successor Prince of Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: De died after six years without an heir; in 120 CE the empress dowager named Liu Yi of Hejian, Marquis of Duxiang, the next Prince of Pingyuan.

sentences.zh: 安帝廢之，國除。

translations.literal: Emperor An deposed him; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor An removed him and abolished the principality.

sentences.zh: 論曰：傳稱吳子夷昧，甚德而度，有吳國者，必其子孫。

translations.literal: The evaluation states: The tradition praises the Wu ruler Yimei as greatly virtuous and measured; whoever holds Wu will surely be his descendant.
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: The Zuo tradition extols Yimei of Wu for virtue and moderation, promising that Wu would pass to his line.

sentences.zh: [一]章帝長者，事從敦厚，繼祀漢室，鹹其苗裔，古人之言信哉！

translations.literal: [One] Emperor Zhang was a man of mature years; affairs followed honest generosity; continuing the sacrifices to the Han house, all are his posterity—how true were the words of the ancients!
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Emperor Zhang ruled with seasoned generosity, and the Han line today runs through his descendants—the old proverb proves itself.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]夷昧，吳君之名。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Yimei is the name of a Wu lord.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Yimei was a ruler of Wu.

sentences.zh: 左傳屈狐庸謂趙文子曰：「若天所啟，其在今嗣君乎？

translations.literal: The Zuo Zhuan: Qu Wuyong said to Zhao Wenzi: 'If Heaven opens the way, is it not in the present successor lord?'
translations.idiomatic: In the Zuo zhuan Qu Wuyong asks Zhao Wenzi whether Heaven's favor rests on Wu's heir.

sentences.zh: 甚德而度，德不失人，度不失事，有吳國者，必此君之子孫也。

translations.literal: Greatly virtuous and measured: in virtue he loses no man, in measure he loses no affair; whoever holds Wu will surely be this lord's descendant.
translations.idiomatic: Yimei is supremely virtuous and judicious—he wrongs no man, misses no duty—and Wu will fall to his seed.'

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云：「嗣君謂夷昧也。」

translations.literal: 'Du Yu's commentary states: 'The successor lord means Yimei.'"
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu identifies the 'successor lord' as Yimei.'"

sentences.zh: 贊曰：章祚不已，本枝流祉。

translations.literal: The encomium states: Zhang's blessing does not cease; root and branch spread blessing.
translations.idiomatic: The encomium: Zhang's blessing endures; trunk and twigs share the grace.

sentences.zh: 質惟伉孫，安亦慶子。

translations.literal: Zhi was Kang's grandson; An was also Qing's son.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhi sprang from Kang's line; Emperor An from Qing's.

sentences.zh: 河閒多福，桓、靈承祀。

translations.literal: Hejian had much good fortune; Huan and Ling received the sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: Hejian's house flourished—Huan and Ling inherited the throne from it.

sentences.zh: 濟北無驕，皇恩寵饒。

translations.literal: Jibei knew no arrogance; imperial grace favored it richly.
translations.idiomatic: Jibei's princes stayed humble and drew lavish favor.

sentences.zh: 平原抱痼，三王薨朝。

translations.literal: Pingyuan clutched chronic illness; three kings died at court.
translations.idiomatic: Pingyuan's line was blighted by sickness; three of its princes died in the capital.

sentences.zh: [一]振振子孫，或秀或苗。

translations.literal: [One] Vibrant are the descendants—some ear, some shoot.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Yet their offspring flourished—some like full grain, some cut short.'

translations.literal: [Two]
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: (marker)

sentences.zh: 注[一]平春王全、廣宗王萬歲、城陽王淑並薨於京師也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Prince Quan of Pingchun, Prince Wansui of Guangzong, and Prince Shu of Chengyang all died in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The three who died at Luoyang were Princes Quan of Pingchun, Wansui of Guangzong, and Shu of Chengyang.

sentences.zh: 注[二]振振，仁厚貌也，音之人反。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Zhen zhen is the appearance of benevolent generosity; the sound is zhi ren fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Zhen zhen describes a thriving, kindly brood; read zhi-ren (平聲).

sentences.zh: 詩國風曰：「宜爾子孫振振兮。

translations.literal: The Classic of Poetry, Airs of the States, states: 'May your descendants be vibrant.'
translations.idiomatic: The Airs say, 'May your children thrive in number.'

sentences.zh: 」論語曰：「苗而不秀者有矣夫，秀而不實者有矣夫！

translations.literal: 'The Analects states: 'There are those who sprout but do not flower; there are those who flower but do not bear fruit!'
translations.idiomatic: The Analects adds, 'Some sprout but never bloom; some bloom yet bear no fruit.'"

sentences.zh: 」苗謂早夭，秀謂成長也。

translations.literal: 'Miao means early death; xiu means growing to maturity.
translations.idiomatic: Here miao means dying young; xiu means living to full growth.'

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes
translations.idiomatic: Textual collation notes

sentences.zh: 一七九七頁二行章帝八王傳第四十五按：集解引黃山說，謂八王中平原王勝既為和帝子，應稱「章和八王」，如前書「宣元六王」之例，「帝」蓋誤字。

translations.literal: Page 1797 line 2, 'Biographies of the Eight Princes of Emperor Zhang,' chapter 45. Collation: The Jijie cites Huang Shan, who observes that since Prince Sheng of Pingyuan was Emperor He's son, the heading ought to read 'The Eight Princes of Emperors Zhang and He,' on the model of the Former Han's 'The Six Princes of Emperors Xuan and Yuan'; the character 'Emperor' is probably an error.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note: Huang Shan argues the chapter title should include He because Liu Sheng was He's son.

sentences.zh: 一七九七頁九行改*[封]*鴻*

translations.literal: Page 1797 line 9: editorial note on emending the phrase about transferring the enfeoffment concerning Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Collation for page 1797, line 9 discusses correcting the wording of Hong's transfer to Bohai.

translations.literal: (feng)
translations.idiomatic: (feng, 'to enfeoff')

sentences.zh: *勃海王校補謂案文「鴻封」當作「封鴻」。

translations.literal: *Prince of Bohai. The collation supplement states that according to the wording 'Hong feng' should read 'feng Hong.'
translations.idiomatic: The supplement argues the word order should be 'enfeoff Hong' rather than 'Hong enfeoff.'

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: The text has been corrected on that basis.

sentences.zh: 一七九八頁一行奉鴻*

translations.literal: Page 1798 line 1: editorial note on the phrase continuing Liu Hong's line.
translations.idiomatic: Collation for page 1798, line 1 glosses the phrase about maintaining Hong's succession.

translations.literal: (si)
translations.idiomatic: (si, 'heir')

sentences.zh: **[祀]*據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: **[ji]* According to the Ji and Palace editions, emended.
translations.idiomatic: The graph for the sacrificial cult is restored per the Ji and Palace recensions.

sentences.zh: 一七九九頁五行太貴人生慶按：集解引惠棟說，謂續漢書云「小貴人」。

translations.literal: Page 1799 line 5: 'the Grand Honored Lady bore Qing.' Collation: The Jijie cites Hui Dong, who says the Xu Han shu reads 'Lesser Honored Lady.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Xu Han shu variant reads xiao guiren instead of tai guiren.

sentences.zh: 一八０一頁六行中傅宦者也按：汲本「宦者」作「官名」。

translations.literal: Page 1801 line 6: 'the palace tutor is a eunuch.' Collation: The Ji edition writes 'office title' instead of 'eunuch.'
translations.idiomatic: Manuscript variant: Ji edition has guan ming where others read huanguan.

sentences.zh: 一八０二頁一二行慶到國下令按：刊誤謂「令」下少一「曰」字。

translations.literal: Page 1802 line 12: 'when Qing arrived in the state he issued an order.' Collation: Kanwu states that yue is missing after ling.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu notes a missing yue ('said') after 'order.'

sentences.zh: 一八０二頁一三行既以薄佑按：「佑」當作「祜」，汲本正作「祜」。

translations.literal: Page 1802 line 13: 'already because of slight you.' Collation: You should be hu; the Ji edition correctly writes hu.
translations.idiomatic: The graph you is a scribal substitute for hu ('blessing'); Ji edition reads hu.

sentences.zh: 然范書「祜」字皆作「佑」，或別有所諱，參閱安帝紀校記。

translations.literal: However, in Fan Ye's History the graph hu is uniformly written you, or there is another taboo involved; see the collation notes to the Annals of Emperor An.
translations.idiomatic: Fan's text consistently writes you for hu; compare the Annals of An.

sentences.zh: 一八０三頁一行續漢*

translations.literal: Page 1803 line 1: Continued Han*
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Page 1803, line 1, on Xu Han—

translations.literal: (shu)
translations.idiomatic: (shu)

sentences.zh: **[志]*曰按：「書」當作「志」，各本皆失正，今改。

translations.literal: **[zhi]* states. Collation: Shu should be zhi; all editions missed the correction; now changed.
translations.idiomatic: treatise) — shu emended to zhi per editorial judgment.

sentences.zh: 一八０三頁六行留慶長子佑集解引惠棟說，謂按說文當作「祜」。

translations.literal: Page 1803 line 6: retaining Qing's eldest son You. The Jijie cites Hui Dong, who says according to the Shuowen it should be written hu.
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong argues the heir's name should be Hu per Shuowen.

sentences.zh: 今按：范書「祜」皆作「佑」，參閱安帝紀校記。

translations.literal: Now note: Fan Ye's History uniformly writes you for hu; see the Annals of Emperor An collation notes.
translations.idiomatic: Same you/hu issue as elsewhere in Fan Ye.

sentences.zh: 一八０四頁一０行*

translations.literal: Page 1804 line 10: manuscript asterisk marking a break in the gloss.
translations.idiomatic: Collation for page 1804, line 10 marks a lacuna before the next gloss fragment.

translations.literal: (tai)
translations.idiomatic: (tai)

sentences.zh: **[大]*宗之義按：殿本考證謂何焯校本「太」改「大」，是。

translations.literal: **[da]* lineage's meaning. Collation: The Palace edition's textual research says He Zhuo's manuscript changes tai to da; this is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Lineage — He Zhuo corrected tai to da; accepted.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Emended accordingly.

sentences.zh: 注同。

translations.literal: Commentary likewise.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary is emended the same way.

sentences.zh: 一八０四頁一一行使司徒持節與大鴻臚奉策書璽綬*[之]*清河校補謂案文「清河」上少一「之」字。

translations.literal: Page 1804 line 11: causing the minister of education bearing credentials together with the grand herald to take the patent, document, and seal-ribbons *[to]* Qinghe. The collation supplement says according to the wording zhi is missing before Qinghe.
translations.idiomatic: Supplement adds zhi before Qinghe: 'to Qinghe.'

sentences.zh: 今據補。

translations.literal: Now supplied accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: The particle has been supplied.

sentences.zh: 一八０五頁四行事已見耿舒傳「已」原作「以」，逕據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: Page 1805 line 4: 'the affair is already seen in Geng Shu's biography' — 'already' originally read 'yi'; directly emended per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Yi changed to yi (already) per editions.

sentences.zh: 按：已以通。

translations.literal: Collation: yi and yi are interchangeable.
translations.idiomatic: Note: the graphs were variant forms.

sentences.zh: 一八０五頁九行*[延平]*立三十五年薨據刊誤補。

translations.literal: Page 1805 line 9: *[Yanping]* having been established thirty-five years died — supplied per Kanwu.
translations.idiomatic: Yanping inserted per Kanwu.

sentences.zh: 一八０五頁一四行甘陵人劉文與南郡妖賊劉鮪交通按：集解引洪頤粻說，謂李固傳「甘陵劉文，魏郡劉鮪，各謀立蒜為天子」。

translations.literal: Page 1805 line 14: 'a man of Ganling named Liu Wen communicated with the sorcerer-rebel Liu Wei of Nan commandery.' Collation: The Jijie cites Hong Yizhang, who says Li Gu's biography has 'Liu Wen of Ganling and Liu Wei of Wei commandery each plotted to establish Suan as Son of Heaven.'
translations.idiomatic: Hong notes Li Gu pairs Ganling with Wei, not Nan commandery.

sentences.zh: 甘陵、魏郡皆與清河近，此作「南郡」，誤。

translations.literal: Ganling and Wei commandery are both near Qinghe; writing 'Nan commandery' here is an error.
translations.idiomatic: Nan is a geographical error.

sentences.zh: 又「劉鮪」朱穆傳作「嚴鮪」。

translations.literal: Moreover 'Liu Wei' in Zhu Mu's biography is written 'Yan Wei.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu's text reads Yan Wei for the surname.

sentences.zh: 一八０六頁一行坐貶爵為尉氏侯徙桂陽按：集解引惠棟說，謂天文志「徙為犍為都鄉侯，薨，國絕」。

translations.literal: Page 1806 line 1: 'sat guilt and was demoted in rank to Marquis of Weishi, transferred to Guiyang.' Collation: The Jijie cites Hui Dong, who says the Treatise on Astronomy reads 'transferred to village marquis of Qianwei commandery; died; state cut off.'
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong cites the astronomical treatise for a different demotion and death.

sentences.zh: 一八０七頁四行立戰鄉侯安國為濟北王按：集解引惠棟說，謂「戰鄉」疑作「闡鄉」。

translations.literal: Page 1807 line 4: establishing Marquis of Zhanxiang Anguo as Prince of Jibei. Collation: The Jijie cites Hui Dong, who says 'Zhanxiang' is suspect for 'Chanxiang.'
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong suggests Chanxiang for Zhanxiang.

sentences.zh: 又引錢大昕說，謂和帝紀封故濟北王壽子安為濟北王，無「國」字。

translations.literal: Moreover it cites Qian Daxin, who says the Annals of He enfeoffed Anguo, son of the former Prince of Jibei Shou, as Prince of Jibei without the word 'state.'
translations.idiomatic: Qian notes He's annals lack guo in the entry.

sentences.zh: 一八０七頁六行安國立*

translations.literal: Page 1807 line 6: Anguo having been established*
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Anguo's reign length—

translations.literal: (ten)
translations.idiomatic: (ten)

sentences.zh: **[七]*年薨張□謂案文「十」當為「七」。

translations.literal: **[seven]* years died — Zhang [lacuna] states that according to the wording 'ten' should be 'seven.'
translations.idiomatic: The editor argues the numeral should read seventeen years to the grave, not a stray ten before seven.

sentences.zh: 質帝紀永嘉元年四月，濟北王安薨，距永和四年止七年耳。

translations.literal: The Basic Annals of Emperor Zhi: fourth month of Yongjia first year, the Prince of Jibei Anguo died — from Yonghe fourth year it is only seven years.
translations.idiomatic: The annals date Anguo's death seven years after his accession.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Emended to seven.

sentences.zh: 一八０七頁一三行次立*[十]*七年薨張森楷校勘記謂次以本初元年嗣，若立七年，當薨於元嘉二年，而本紀於延熹五年乃有次薨之文，則相距十七年矣，「七」上明奪「十」字。

translations.literal: Page 1807 line 13: Ci having been established *[ten]* seventeen years died — Zhang Senkai's collation states Ci succeeded in Benchu first year; if he reigned seven years he would have died in Yuanjia second year, yet the basic annals only record Ci's death in Yanxi fifth year — seventeen years apart; clearly 'ten' is missing above 'seven.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Senkai's collation argues that Ci succeeded in the first year of Benchu, so a reign of only seven years would place his death in the second year of Yuanjia, yet the basic annals record his death only in the fifth year of Yanxi—seventeen years later—proving that the graph for 'ten' was lost before 'seven' in the received text.

sentences.zh: 今據補。

translations.literal: Now supplied accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: The emendation is adopted.

sentences.zh: 一八０八頁二行永寧元年至奉樂成王黨祀按：集解引錢大昕說，謂安帝紀是年與平原王同封者，乃濟北王壽之子樂成王萇也。

translations.literal: Page 1808 lines 2ff.: Yongning first year through 'continuing the sacrifices to Prince of Lecheng Dang.' Collation: The Jijie cites Qian Daxin, who says the Annals of Emperor An for that year together enfeoffed with the Prince of Pingyuan the Prince of Lecheng Chang, son of Shou of Jibei.
translations.idiomatic: Qian notes the parallel entry names Chang of Lecheng, not De.

sentences.zh: 其明年為建光元年，鄧太后崩，樂成王萇亦以罪廢。

translations.literal: The next year is Jianguang first year; the empress dowager Deng died, and Prince of Lecheng Chang was also deposed for crime.
translations.idiomatic: Chang was deposed the next year.

sentences.zh: 又明年為延光元年，始改樂成國為安平，封河閒王開子得為王，得與德本一人也。

translations.literal: The next year is Yanguang first year; only then was the state of Lecheng changed to Ping'an, and Prince Kai of Hejian's son De (homophone 'obtain') was enfeoffed as prince — that man and the one elsewhere called De ('virtue') were originally one person.
translations.idiomatic: In Yanguang the Lecheng domain became Ping'an; Qian Daxin shows the prince called De in the annals is the same figure elsewhere written with the homophone meaning 'virtue.'

sentences.zh: 此傳蓋有脫文，不可考矣。

translations.literal: This biography probably has lacunae and cannot be fully reconstructed.
translations.idiomatic: The received text is defective here.

sentences.zh: 一八０八頁八行王不服按：刊誤謂「服」上少一「王」字。

translations.literal: Page 1808 line 8: 'the king will not submit.' Collation: Kanwu says 'king' is missing above fu.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu adds a second wang before fu.

sentences.zh: 一九０九頁四行中大夫趙王按：集解引惠棟說，謂蔣果云「中大夫」疑當作「中大人」。

translations.literal: Page 1909 line 4: 'Palace Grandee Wang of Zhao.' Collation: The Jijie cites Hui Dong, who says Jiang Guo holds 'palace grandee' should be 'palace elder' (zhong daren).
translations.idiomatic: Possible emendation zhong daren for zhong dafu.

sentences.zh: 又殿本考證謂「王」字疑當作「玉」，鄧太后紀有宮人趙玉。

translations.literal: Moreover the Palace edition's textual research says the graph wang is suspect for yu; the Annals of Empress Dowager Deng have a palace woman Zhao Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Wang may be the woman Zhao Yu.

sentences.zh: 一八０九頁一０行更封帝*

translations.literal: Page 1809 line 10: editorial note on the phrase again enfeoffing the emperor's kinsman, with manuscript break.
translations.idiomatic: Collation for page 1809, line 10 concerns redistributing the Pingyuan title among the emperor's close kin.

translations.literal: (elder brother)
translations.idiomatic: (elder brother)

sentences.zh: **[弟]*都鄉侯碩為平原王按：「兄」當依桓帝紀作「弟」。

translations.literal: **[younger brother]* Marquis of Duxiang Shuo as Prince of Pingyuan. Collation: 'Elder brother' should follow the Annals of Emperor Huan as 'younger brother.'
translations.idiomatic: Xiong should be di per Huan's annals.

sentences.zh: 桓帝紀校補引侯康說，謂東觀記稱桓帝為蠡吾侯長子，則帝不得有兄也。

translations.literal: The collation supplement to Huan's annals cites Hou Kang: the Eastern Lodge Record calls Emperor Huan the eldest son of Marquis of Liyi; therefore the emperor could not have had an elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Kang cites Dongguan ji: Huan was eldest son of Liyi—no elder brother.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Emended to younger brother.

sentences.zh: 一八０九頁一三行子*

translations.literal: Page 1809 line 13: editorial note on the heir's name with manuscript break.
translations.idiomatic: Collation for page 1809, line 13 identifies the successor of Marquis Shu.

translations.literal: (chang)
translations.idiomatic: (chang)

sentences.zh: **[萇]*嗣刊誤謂案紀「長」作「萇」，他書亦然，明此誤。

translations.literal: **[chang]* succeeded. Kanwu states that according to the annals 'chang' is written chang; other books likewise; clearly this is an error.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu shows the annals spell the heir's name with the 苌 graph; editors adopt that reading.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Emended.

sentences.zh: 一八一０頁二行康為濟南王按：集解引錢大昕說，謂案光武子有濟南安王康，此濟南王亦名康，先後同國同名，亦可疑也。

translations.literal: Page 1810 line 2: 'Kang as Prince of Jinan.' Collation: The Jijie cites Qian Daxin, who says Emperor Guangwu's son had Prince Kang of Jinan—this Prince of Jinan also named Kang is suspicious for sharing state and name.
translations.idiomatic: Qian flags duplicate name Kang for Jinan.

sentences.zh: 御覽引續漢書，此濟南王名庾。

translations.literal: The Imperial Digest citing the Xu Han shu gives this Prince of Jinan the name Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Yulan's Xu Han shu reads Yu instead of Kang.
